title: Delivered an AI assistant
subTitle: Enabling users to get quick answers to their questions
caption: The release management AI assistant that I delivered
bullets: Some IBMers were struggling with the many internal IBM release management processes. To help such users, I configured and deployed an AI assistant.
I used the IBM watsonx Orchestrate platform to connect the assistant to a documentation set that I had written.
The AI assistant is embedded directly in the internal release management application that Product Managers and other colleagues already use.
The AI assistant responds to users' natural language questions, providing a summary response as well as links to further details.

title: Created a Figma plugin that checks content
subTitle: Helping IBM designers easily check and improve the written aspects of their designs
imageAlt: Screenshot of the content checker Figma plugin
caption: The content checker Figma plugin that I helped create for IBM designers
bullets: While we have good content guidelines at IBM, it's easy for designers to forget aspects, sometimes leading to poor UI content.
I came up with the idea of providing them with a way to check the written content of their product UI designs directly within Figma.
Some colleagues helped me get a prototype AI-powered Figma plugin running and I then worked on providing the content rules for the AI model to reference.
I iteratively tested the plugin on example Figma designs and refined the content guidelines and LLM settings until the plugin was ready to publish.
The Figma plugin is now being used by over 200 IBM designers, helping them perfect their UI content. 🎯

title: Writing UI content
subTitle: Working with product teams to deliver great content
imageAlt: Screenshot of the IBM Event Streams product UI
caption: An example: the IBM Event Streams product UI that I worked on
bullets: I work with lots of different IBM product teams. For example, I joined the IBM Event Streams team for a period of 5 months.
I ran a content audit to help the team review what content they had, what user needs it addressed, and what needed removing, updating, or creating.
After learning about the target users, I wrote all of the UI content for the new features the team were working on.
I helped the team design a new set of [[marketing pages|https://www.ibm.com/products/event-streams]] that articulated how the product addressed common user goals.
We won external design awards 🏆 for both the product itself and the [[docs site|https://ibm.github.io/event-automation/es/]].

title: Setting content standards
subTitle: Providing guidance to IBM designers on all aspects of product content
imageAlt: Screenshot of the content guidance section within the Carbon for IBM Products site
caption: The content guidance section that I wrote and maintain
bullets: Back in 2016, I was asked to produce IBM Design's first voice and tone guide. I started with thorough research to understand what aspects of writing designers and engineers were struggling with. I've written about this process in [[this article|https://medium.com/design-ibm/confessions-of-a-content-designer-9be86daca04]].
I then focused on how we could best design and deliver the guidance so that it would be fun, useful, and easy to use.

When IBM adopted the [[Carbon Design System|https://www.carbondesignsystem.com/]] for all product UIs, I began contributing to both [[the brief external|https://www.carbondesignsystem.com/guidelines/content/overview]] and more extensive internal content guides.
This is something that I and others continue to evolve — Carbon is used by hundreds of IBM product teams, as well as many outside of IBM.

title: Researching and testing terminology
subTitle: Ensuring that the words we use communicate clearly with our target users
imageAlt: Examples from the terminology cloze test that I ran
caption: Examples from the terminology cloze test that I ran
bullets: One IBM product team was showing potential customers early product designs and noticed confusion around some of the terms being used.
The team were divided about which terms to use, so I was asked to help resolve these terminology issues.
I designed and ran a 'cloze test' research study where recruited target users were given a series of sentences and asked to supply missing words.
This research provided the team with empirical data showing which terms resonated with the target audience and which did not.
This enabled the team to make informed decisions about which terms to use, based on real evidence about their users' mental models.

title: Delivering content education
subTitle: Creating a UX writing course for all IBM designers
imageAlt: Screenshot of the IBM UX writing course
caption: The IBM UX writing course that I published in late 2022
bullets: IBM has a community of over 3,000 designers. While we have dedicated content designers, many design teams don't have access to a content specialist.
In 2022, I was asked to create a self-service online course to help UX designers, visual designers, and design researchers improve their UX writing skills.
After getting input from a variety of design colleagues, I came up with a course outline that mapped to both our Carbon content guidelines and internal design review heuristics.
As I drafted each module I sought feedback from early adopters, enabling me to refine the content until it was ready for publication.

title: Providing content assessments
subTitle: Helping product teams to further improve their UI content
imageAlt: Examples of the detailed content reviews I provide to product teams
caption: Examples of the detailed content reviews I provide to product teams
bullets: Sadly, many IBM product teams do not have a dedicated content specialist.
While they have content guidance to follow, their UI content often has scope for improvement.
Before being released, all products go through a formal 'Design & User Experience' review and I often provide the content-specific part of this assessment.

I assess the written parts of the product UI against a set of content heuristics and provide detailed feedback highlighting both strengths and areas for improvement, along with suggestions and supporting materials.

title: Helping to create a spark
subTitle: Writing the comms for the internal 2023 IBM Spark Design Festival
imageAlt: The website for the internal IBM Spark Design Festival 2023
caption: The website for the internal IBM Spark Design Festival 2023
bullets: For the last few years, IBM has run a fantastic annual design festival featuring presentations, lightning talks, panel discussions, and workshops.
I joined the leadership committee in 2023, responsible for all festival content and communications.
I worked closely with committee members, speakers, and volunteers to ensure all festival information was communicated clearly and on-brand.
This involved writing website copy, event emails, and many Slack announcements before, during, and after the festival.
A colleague and I also started a monthly Spark blog, each edition exploring one design topic and featuring talks from the Spark archives.

title: Establishing a community of practice
subTitle: Bringing together colleagues who have a passion for great content
imageAlt: Screenshot of our IBM Content Design Guild Slack channel
caption: Our IBM Content Design Guild Slack channel
bullets: Back in 2018, many people in IBM hadn't even heard of a content designer, let alone worked with one.
So I got together with the talented [[Maranda Bodas|https://www.linkedin.com/in/marandabodas/]] and a handful of others, and we formed a small community of practice: the IBM Content Design Guild.
From an initial meeting with just 7 of us, our community has grown month by month — we now have 50+ people at our monthly meetings and 500+ in our Slack channel.
At our lively meetings we share best practices and resources, run content crits, and learn about different aspects of content design and strategy.
You can read more about how we established this vibrant community in [[this article|https://uxdesign.cc/building-a-community-of-practice-859db7950f77]].
